title: Texture transform rotation is applied in an inconsistent order wrt glTF
author: zeux
body: ### Description three.js uses an inconsistent order of internal operations when composing texture transformation matrix. Here and below I will assume "consistent" means "TRS": scale is applied first, followed by rotation, followed by translation. This is the order glTF specified KHR_texture_transform in, see https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/main/extensions/2.0/Khronos/KHR_texture_transform/README.md, and it's also the order that matches the 3D transform representation. From KHR_texture_transform spec: > mat3 matrix = translation * rotation * scale; > vec2 uvTransformed = ( matrix * vec3(Uv.xy, 1) ).xy; three.js uses Matrix3.setUvTransform which reduces to (for center=0): ``` [ sx*c sx*s tx ] [ -sy*s sy*c ty ] [ 0 0 1 ] ``` instead of: ``` [ sx*c -sy*s tx ] [ sx*s sy*c ty ] [ 0 0 1 ] ``` When scale is uniform (repeat.x = repeat.y), this does not matter: S * R = R * S. However, when scale is not uniform, this results in the UVs being scaled *after* rotation, instead of being scaled in the rotated frame, along the direction of rotation. ### Reproduction steps Open the attached GLB file in a three.js viewer [ttmtf.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29974248/ttmtf.zip) ### Code - ### Live example - ### Screenshots three.js (via https://gltf-viewer.donmccurdy.com/): Babylon.js (via https://sandbox.babylonjs.com/): ### Version r176 ### Device _No response_ ### Browser _No response_ ### OS _No response_

title: `WorkgroupInfoNode`: `scope` parameter/property undocumented (JSDoc TODO)
author: LalitNarayanYadav
body:
### Description

`WorkgroupInfoNode`'s constructor and `.scope` property both have their JSDoc description left as `TODO.` in `src/nodes/gpgpu/WorkgroupInfoNode.js` (lines 74 and 114). This carries through unchanged to the generated docs page (WorkgroupInfoNode.html on threejs.org/docs), so it's a live gap on the public API reference, not just a source comment.

From the only usage in the codebase, `workgroupArray()` (line 232), `scope` is passed the string `'Workgroup'` and is used to build the generated buffer name (`${this.scope}Array`) and to look up the scoped array via `builder.getScopedArray(name, this.scope.toLowerCase(), ...)`. Right now there's no explanation of what values `scope` accepts or whether it maps to WGSL address space qualifiers.

### Solution

Fill in the JSDoc for `scope` in `src/nodes/gpgpu/WorkgroupInfoNode.js` explaining what it represents and its accepted values (e.g. confirm whether `'Workgroup'` is the only valid value in practice, or if other WGSL scopes like `'Private'` are ever passed). Happy to submit a PR once a maintainer confirms the intended semantics.

### Alternatives

None, this is a documentation-only gap, not a design decision, so there isn't a real alternative approach.

### Additional context

Related TODOs in the same area, could be bundled into this issue or filed separately:
- `NodeMaterial.outputNode` - "TODO: Explain the differences to `fragmentNode`." (docs/pages/NodeMaterial.html.md)
- `TSL.textureStore()` - "TODO: Explain difference to `storageTexture()`." (docs/pages/TSL.html.md)

title: getParallaxCorrectNormal computes the box bounds incorrectly when cubePos ≠ (0,0,0)
author: alkan212
body:
`getParallaxCorrectNormal()` builds the projection box corners as `±0.5 · (cubeSize − cubePos)`:

```js
// src/nodes/functions/material/getParallaxCorrectNormal.js
const rbmax = sub( float( 0.5 ).mul( cubeSize.sub( cubePos ) ), positionWorld ).div( nDir );
```

The corners of a box of size `cubeSize` centered at `cubePos` should be `cubePos ± 0.5 · cubeSize`.
The two only match when `cubePos = (0,0,0)`.

With the values from the function's own JSDoc (`cubeSize (200,100,100)`, `cubePos (0,−50,0)` —
a room whose ceiling is at y = 0), a ray toward the ceiling exits at **y = +75** instead of y = 0,
so reflections aim at walls that aren't there. The `webgpu_materials_envmaps_bpcem` example uses
these same values.

The pre-TSL example this function reimplements (`webgl_materials_envmaps_parallax.html`,
removed in #22369) had it right:

```glsl
vec3 rbmax = ( .5 * cubeSize + cubePos - vWorldPosition ) / nDir;
```

The `+ cubePos` moved inside the parentheses as `− cubePos` during the TSL port (#29773).

Suggested fix (restores the original math):

```js
const rbmax = cubePos.add( cubeSize.mul( 0.5 ) ).sub( positionWorld ).div( nDir ).toVar();
const rbmin = cubePos.sub( cubeSize.mul( 0.5 ) ).sub( positionWorld ).div( nDir ).toVar();
```

Present in r184, r185 (latest release) and current `dev`.

---

*Note: this bug was found and this report written by an AI (Claude), reviewed by me before
posting. If it turns out to be wrong, I'll stop posting AI-assisted reports.*

title: TiledLightsNode: all point lights silently go dark when scene.environment (or lightMap/AO) is set — getLights() omits the diverted point lights
author: alkan212
body:
**Description**

`TiledLightsNode.setLights()` diverts point lights into its internal tiled list and passes only the remaining lights to `super.setLights( materialLights )`. The class does **not** override `getLights()`, so it reports only the non-point lights.

`NodeMaterial.setupLights()` rebuilds a per-material lights node from `lightsNode.getLights()` whenever the material has environment / lightMap / AO contributions — e.g. the moment `scene.environment` is assigned. The rebuilt node therefore receives **zero point lights**, and every point light silently goes dark on all standard materials (ambient/directional survive, which makes it look like a scene-specific lighting bug). Setting `scene.environment = null` restores them. No warning or error is produced.

**Reproduction steps**

1. `WebGPURenderer` + `renderer.lighting = new TiledLighting()`.
2. Add a `PointLight` — the floor pool lights up.
3. `scene.environment = <any PMREM texture>` — the point-light contribution disappears.
4. `scene.environment = null` — it returns.

**Suggested fix (verified downstream, two parts)**

1. Override `getLights()` to return the full set (base list + the diverted tiled lights).
2. Make re-wrapped per-material nodes **delegate their tile cull to the scene-level node** (guarded by frame/render id) instead of each building their own compute pass — with (1) alone, every standard material instantiates its own cull compute + buffers. With delegation there is exactly one cull per scene and light add/remove stays buffer-driven. Implemented this way, probe/light in both orders and live `scene.environment` toggles restore the frame bit-identically.

**Live example**

None yet — the root cause is code-level. Happy to build a fiddle if useful.

**Version**

r184, WebGPURenderer/TSL.

title: TiledLightsNode: point-light specular is wrong — mat4.mul(vec3) upcasts the light vector to vec4 and w corrupts BRDF_GGX
author: alkan212
body:
**Description**

In `examples/jsm/tsl/lighting/TiledLightsNode.js`, `getLightData()` computes the light's view-space position as:

```js
const viewPosition = this._cameraViewMatrix.mul( position ); // position = dataA.xyz (vec3)
```

In TSL, `mat4.mul(vec3)` upcasts the vec3 operand to `vec4(v, 1.0)` and **returns a vec4**. That vec4 then flows into the shading loop:

```js
directPointLight( { ..., lightVector: viewPosition.sub( positionView ), ... } );
```

so `lightVector` is vec4-typed. Inside `BRDF_GGX` the half-vector math mixes vec4 and vec3 operands (`positionViewDirection` upcasts with `w = 1`), so `dotNH` / `dotVH` pick up a non-zero `w` contribution and the **specular term becomes geometry-dependently wrong**. Diffuse is unaffected, which hides the bug on metallic test scenes but makes it large on rough dielectrics: measured on a `roughness 1 / metalness 0` floor lit by one `PointLight`, the per-light contribution (lit minus baseline, linear) was **52–76% of the forward path**, with the ratio varying by view geometry.

**Suggested fix (verified downstream)**

```js
const viewPosition = this._cameraViewMatrix.mul( vec4( position, 1.0 ) ).xyz;
```

With this one change our downstream copy of the node re-measures **bit-identical to the forward path** at every probe point and channel (8-bit readback limit).

**Reproduction steps**

1. `WebGPURenderer`, `renderer.lighting = new TiledLighting()`.
2. One `PointLight` above a `MeshStandardMaterial` plane (`roughness: 1, metalness: 0`).
3. Read any lit pixel and compare with the identical scene under default forward lighting — values differ, and the delta changes with camera angle.

**Live example**

None yet — the root cause is code-level (exact lines above). Happy to build a fiddle if useful.

**Version**

r184, WebGPURenderer/TSL.

title: `renderer.compile(..)` leaks mesh with WeakMap
author: ycw
body:
### Description

When a mesh is used as a WeakMap key, it should be released once it goes out of scope and its geometry/material are disposed. Testing confirms this expected behavior. But when pre‑compiling mesh with `renderer.compile(..)`, a hidden strong reference seems to hold the mesh, preventing GC. As a result, the mesh and its associated data remain permanently retained in the WeakMap.


### Reproduction steps

1. go to https://codepen.io/ycw/pen/NPdyjKm
2. open console (F12)
3. trigger GC (Shift+Ctrl+p > 'collect garbage')
4. observe weakmap state, the mesh is still retained on the weakmap

title: Line2: Add TAA support.
author: Ryan-G7
body: ### Description I found an issue where TRAA causes unexpected blurring effects on Line2 (and related wide lines like LineSegments2) during camera interactions. Cause: When TRAA calculates velocity vectors using velocityNode, it does not correctly process or transform the specific layout and vertex attributes of Line2. Because the motion vectors for these expanded line vertices aren't calculated accurately relative to the camera's movement, the velocity buffer receives incorrect data, leading to aggressive blurring during camera manipulation. I have already identified the fix and submitted a PR to address this! ### Reproduction steps 1. Create a scene containing Line2 or LineSegments2 geometry. linewidth 1.1 2. Enable TRAA with velocity-based motion vectors. 3. Rotate or pan the camera interactively. ### Code ```js // code goes here ``` ### Live example // ... ### Screenshots blurring: normal: ### Version r185 ### Device Desktop ### Browser Chrome ### OS Windows

title: WebGLRenderer: `CompressedArrayTexture` layer update is slow if mipmap are present
author: agargaro
body:
### Description

Hi!

When performing partial layer updates on a `CompressedArrayTexture`, the update is not done correctly if the texture has mipmaps. Cause [here ](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLTextures.js#L1068)the call to `texture.clearLayerUpdates()` is inside the mipmap loop instead of being called once after all mip uploads, so the "layer updated" state is reset incorrectly, updating all mipmaps instead of just the one for the specified layer.

I can make a PR moving the `texture.clearLayerUpdates()` statement outside the loop if it's okay for you.



### Reproduction steps

- Create a CompressedTextureArray with the maximum possible length.
- Update a single texture every frame

### Code

```js
arrayTexture.addLayerUpdate(currentLayerToUpdate);
arrayTexture.needsUpdate = true;
```

### Live example

https://jsfiddle.net/wtcx1nyo/3/

example with texture 256x256 

### Screenshots

_No response_

### Version

r185

### Device

Desktop

### Browser

Chrome

### OS

Windows

title: Fog calculation does not consider depthNode value
author: pohlt
body: ### Description The fog calculation does not seem to consider an explicitly set `depthNode` value. Context: I try to use this for volume rendering using ray marching. I managed to get correct Phong shading by setting the `normalNode` and correct interaction with other objects by setting the `depthNode` value, but the fog still is an issue. ### Reproduction steps 1. Use any Node-based material (e.g. `MeshBasicNodeMaterial`) 2. Set `depthNode` of the material to a value in the front. 3. Set up fog. Expected behavior: The fog is applied according to the depth value. In this case the fog should not be visible. Actual behavior: The fog is calculated is if `depthNode` hadn't been modified. ### Code ```js // material const material = new THREE.MeshBasicNodeMaterial( { side: THREE.BackSide } ); // setting all fragments to depth 0.9 material.depthNode = 0.9 // mesh mesh = new THREE.Mesh( geometry, material ); scene.add( mesh ); // fog scene.fog = new THREE.Fog(0x040404, 0, 45) ``` ### Live example https://jsfiddle.net/bm1poeka/8/ ### Screenshots ### Version r185 ### Device Desktop ### Browser Chrome ### OS Linux

title: TSLGraphLoader does not resolve bare three imports in inspector TSL graph payloads
author: bhushan6
body: ## Summary `TSLGraphLoader` in the inspector resolves `three/tsl` imports, but it does not resolve bare `three` imports. This breaks TSL graph payloads that inline helpers relying on named `three` imports such as `Color`, `Vector2`, or `Vector3`. ## Current behavior In our web app the same exported graph works, but in the Three.js inspector it fails during material parsing/execution. Observed errors: ```text Inspector.js:227 THREE.TSLGraph: Library three not found. VM53:158 Uncaught (in promise) ReferenceError: Color is not defined at eval (eval at parseMaterial (TSLGraphLoader.js:93:23), :158:19) at materials.tslGraph (eval at parseMaterial (TSLGraphLoader.js:93:23), :193:8) at TSLGraphEditor._updateMaterial (TSLGraphEditor.js:851:24) at async TSLGraphEditor.onEvent (TSLGraphEditor.js:367:4) ``` ## Why it happens The inspector loader currently does this: ```js import * as THREE from 'three'; import * as TSL from 'three/tsl'; const _library = { 'three/tsl': { ...TSL } }; ``` So when the exported graph payload includes: ```json { "from": "three", "imports": ["Color", "Vector2", "Vector3"] } ``` those imports are not resolved by the loader, even though `THREE` is already imported in the module. Then the generated code later hits expressions like `new Color(...)` and fails with `ReferenceError: Color is not defined`. ## Expected behavior If `TSLGraphLoader` already imports `* as THREE from 'three'`, it should also expose bare `three` named imports through `_library`, just like it already does for `three/tsl`. ## Proposed fix Update `examples/jsm/inspector/extensions/tsl-graph/TSLGraphLoader.js` to include bare `three`: ```js import * as THREE from 'three'; import * as TSL from 'three/tsl'; const _library = { 'three': { ...THREE }, 'three/tsl': { ...TSL } }; ``` ### Reproduction steps 1. Open the Three.js inspector with TSL Graph enabled. 2. Create or select a NodeMaterial-based material that is editable through TSL Graph. 3. Open the TSL Graph editor for that material. 4. Add a Camouflage node. 5. Connect the color output of the Camouflage node to the material color input. 6. Let the inspector compile/apply the graph. ### Screenshots ### Version 0.185.0 ### Device _No response_ ### Browser _No response_ ### OS _No response_

title: XRManager (WebGPURenderer): "Failed to construct 'XRWebGLBinding': parameter 1 is not of type 'XRSession'" with polyfilled WebXR sessions (Meta Immersive Web Emulator) — regression of #31432
author: jjmhalew
body:
### Description

**NOTE:** While trying to reimplement VR in my project (since I saw r185 now supports WebXR WebGPU) with WebGL fallback, I ran into some issues. I asked **Claude Fable (High)** and it recommended me to report these issues as bugs and made the description/reproduction steps/examples)

The new `XRManager` used by `WebGPURenderer` decides whether to use the `XRProjectionLayer` path purely from the presence of the **native** `XRWebGLBinding` API ([src/renderers/common/XRManager.js](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/src/renderers/common/XRManager.js)):

```js
this._supportsGlBinding = typeof XRWebGLBinding !== 'undefined';
...
this._supportsLayers = ( this._supportsGlBinding && 'createProjectionLayer' in XRWebGLBinding.prototype );
```

and later constructs the binding without any guard:

```js
this._glBinding = new XRWebGLBinding( this._session, this._gl );
```

When the XR session comes from **webxr-polyfill** (used by the Meta [Immersive Web Emulator](https://github.com/meta-quest/immersive-web-emulator) browser extension — the standard desktop WebXR dev workflow), the global `XRWebGLBinding` is Chrome's native class but the session is a polyfill object. The native constructor's WebIDL type check rejects it:

```
TypeError: Failed to construct 'XRWebGLBinding': parameter 1 is not of type 'XRSession'.
    at XRManager.setSession
```

so `setSession()` throws and the session can never start. This makes the WebGL-backend XR path of `WebGPURenderer` untestable with the emulator.

This is the same failure mode as #31432, which was reported for the classic `WebXRManager` (r173 regression) and resolved for the r179 milestone — the new `XRManager` reintroduces it.

Note for the fix: forcing the `XRWebGLLayer` fallback path is not enough by itself, because `foveateBoundTexture()` → `getBinding()` also constructs `XRWebGLBinding` lazily per frame; the polyfilled-session case needs both `_supportsLayers` and `_supportsGlBinding` to resolve to `false` (or `getBinding()` needs a try/catch). A session-level decision (e.g. try/catch with fallback to the `XRWebGLLayer` path, or checking `session instanceof XRSession`) would restore the classic manager's behavior.

### Reproduction steps

1. Install the Meta Immersive Web Emulator extension in Chrome (desktop, no headset).
2. Run the code below (WebGL backend, since the emulator cannot grant the `webgpu` session feature at all).
3. Open DevTools → WebXR emulator tab, then click **ENTER VR**.
4. Browser Console shows `TypeError: Failed to construct 'XRWebGLBinding': parameter 1 is not of type 'XRSession'` from `XRManager.setSession`; the session never starts.
5. Same setup with the classic `WebGLRenderer` (r179+) enters VR fine.

### Code

Demo code
```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <script type="importmap">
    {
      "imports": {
        "three/webgpu": "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three@0.185.0/build/three.webgpu.js",
        "three/addons/": "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three@0.185.0/examples/jsm/"
      }
    }
  </script>
</head>
<body>
  <script type="module">
    import * as THREE from "three/webgpu";
    import { VRButton } from "three/addons/webxr/VRButton.js";

    const renderer = new THREE.WebGPURenderer({ forceWebGL: true });
    renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);
    renderer.xr.enabled = true;
    document.body.appendChild(renderer.domElement);
    document.body.appendChild(VRButton.createButton(renderer));

    const scene = new THREE.Scene();
    scene.add(new THREE.Mesh(new THREE.BoxGeometry(), new THREE.MeshBasicMaterial()));

    const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(50, window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight, 0.1, 10);
    camera.position.set(0, 1.6, 3);

    renderer.setAnimationLoop(() => renderer.render(scene, camera));
  </script>
</body>
</html>
```


### Live example

* [jsfiddle-dev WebGPURenderer](https://jsfiddle.net/jjmhalew/0v5wekso/2/)


### Screenshots

_No response_

### Version

r185.1

### Device

Desktop

### Browser

Chrome

### OS

Windows
